question_text: Match the elements of Marketing Mix in Column I with their components in Column II:

column_b.text: Advertising, personal selling, sales promotion, publicity
Channels of distribution, warehousing, transportation
Branding, packaging, labelling, product design
Cost-plus pricing, penetration, skimming, discounts

explanation: (1) Product = Branding, packaging, labelling (c). (2) Price = Pricing strategies and discounts (d). (3) Place = Distribution channels, warehousing (b). (4) Promotion = Advertising, selling, publicity (a).
subject: cuet-business-studies

elimination_hints.hint: Product is branding/packaging (c), not advertising (a). Advertising belongs to Promotion.
Price is pricing strategies (d), not distribution (b). Place is distribution (b).
Product is branding (c), not pricing (d). This reverses Product and Price.
elimination_hints.misconception: Completely mixing up all four elements

question_text: Match the promotional tools in Column I with their key features in Column II:

column_b.text: Short-term incentives like discounts, samples, contests
Unpaid, non-personal communication through media coverage
Paid, non-personal mass communication via media
Direct, face-to-face interaction with buyer

explanation: (1) Advertising = Paid, non-personal, mass media (c). (2) Personal Selling = Face-to-face (d). (3) Sales Promotion = Short-term incentives (a). (4) Publicity = Unpaid media coverage (b).
subject: cuet-business-studies

elimination_hints.hint: Advertising is paid (c), not unpaid (b). Publicity is unpaid. Don't swap advertising and publicity.
Personal Selling is face-to-face (d), not short-term incentives (a). Sales Promotion offers incentives.
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping personal selling and sales promotion

elimination_hints.hint: Advertising is mass media (c), not face-to-face (d). This swaps advertising and personal selling.

question_text: Match the channels of distribution in Column I with the number of intermediaries in Column II:

column_b.text: Manufacturer → Wholesaler → Retailer → Consumer
Manufacturer → Agent → Wholesaler → Retailer → Consumer
Manufacturer → Consumer (no intermediary)
Manufacturer → Retailer → Consumer

explanation: (1) Zero-level = Direct, no intermediary (c). (2) One-level = 1 intermediary — Retailer (d). (3) Two-level = 2 intermediaries — Wholesaler + Retailer (a). (4) Three-level = 3 intermediaries — Agent + Wholesaler + Retailer (b).
subject: cuet-business-studies

elimination_hints.hint: One-level has only a retailer (d), not wholesaler + retailer (a). Don't swap one-level and two-level.
Zero-level is direct/no intermediary (c), not through retailer (d). Don't swap zero and one-level.
Two-level is Wholesaler + Retailer (a), not Agent + Wholesaler + Retailer (b). Don't swap two and three-level.

question_text: Match the pricing strategies in Column I with their descriptions in Column II:

column_b.text: Adding a fixed markup to the cost of production
Setting a low price initially to gain rapid market share
Setting price just below a round number (e.g., Rs 499)
Setting a high initial price for an innovative product

explanation: (1) Penetration = Low price for market share (b). (2) Skimming = High initial price (d). (3) Psychological = Just below round number (c). (4) Cost-plus = Markup on cost (a).
subject: cuet-business-studies

elimination_hints.hint: Penetration is LOW price (b), not HIGH (d). Skimming is HIGH, not LOW. These are opposites.
Psychological pricing is the 'just below round number' trick (c), not cost-plus markup (a).
Penetration is low price for market share (b), not odd pricing (c).

question_text: Match the marketing concepts in Column I with their starting points in Column II:

column_b.text: Factory — produce first, then use aggressive selling
Market — identify customer needs, then satisfy them
Factory — focus on mass production and efficiency
Product — focus on quality and continuous improvement

explanation: (1) Production = Mass production & efficiency (c). (2) Product = Quality improvement (d). (3) Selling = Aggressive promotion (a). (4) Marketing = Customer needs (b).
subject: cuet-business-studies

elimination_hints.hint: Production concept is about efficiency (c), not aggressive selling (a). Selling concept uses aggressive promotion.
Product concept focuses on quality (d), not customer needs (b). Marketing concept focuses on customer needs.
Selling concept starts from factory with aggressive selling (a), not market-based (b). Marketing concept is market-based.
